title: Multipartite Entanglement Quiz
description: Test your understanding of multipartite entanglement including GHZ states, W states, SLOCC classification, and graph states.

questions.question: What happens to a 3-qubit GHZ state when one qubit is lost (traced out)?
options: The remaining two qubits are still maximally entangled
The remaining two qubits are in a partially entangled state
The remaining two qubits are in a classical mixture (separable)
The state becomes undefined
questions.explanation: The GHZ state is fragile: if any single qubit is lost, the remaining two qubits are left in a classical mixture of |00><00| and |11><11|, which is completely separable. This is a distinctive property that distinguishes GHZ from W states.

questions.question: The 3-qubit W state |W> = (|100> + |010> + |001>)/sqrt(3) has what property when one qubit is lost?
options: All entanglement is destroyed
The remaining two qubits remain entangled
The state becomes a product state
The remaining qubits become maximally entangled
questions.explanation: The W state is robust to qubit loss. When one qubit is traced out, the remaining two qubits are still entangled (though not maximally). This robustness makes W states useful for quantum communication scenarios where qubit loss may occur.

questions.question: In SLOCC classification for three qubits, the GHZ and W states belong to:
options: The same SLOCC class
Different SLOCC classes that cannot be inter-converted even probabilistically
Classes that can be converted with 50% success probability
Biseparable classes
questions.explanation: GHZ and W states represent two distinct and inequivalent SLOCC classes for three qubits. No local operation, even probabilistic, can convert one to the other. This reflects fundamentally different types of multipartite entanglement.

questions.question: How is a graph state |G> constructed from a graph G = (V, E)?
options: Apply CNOT gates for each edge between qubits initialized in |0>
Apply CZ gates for each edge between qubits initialized in |+>
Apply Hadamard gates for each vertex, then SWAP for each edge
Create a GHZ state and apply local rotations based on edge structure
questions.explanation: Graph states are constructed by: (1) initializing all qubits in |+> = (|0> + |1>)/sqrt(2), then (2) applying a controlled-Z (CZ) gate for each edge in the graph. The CZ gate creates the characteristic correlations of graph states.

questions.question: What is genuine multipartite entanglement (GME)?
options: Entanglement shared equally among all parties
Entanglement that cannot be expressed as a mixture of biseparable states
The maximum possible entanglement for n parties
Entanglement that survives any single qubit loss
questions.explanation: A state has genuine multipartite entanglement if it cannot be written as a convex combination of states that are separable across some bipartition. GME means all parties are genuinely quantum-correlated, not just pairwise.

questions.question: The three-tangle is a measure that:
options: Measures the total entanglement in a three-qubit state
Distinguishes GHZ-type entanglement (tau > 0) from W-type (tau = 0)
Counts the number of Bell pairs that can be distilled
Measures the fidelity with the nearest GHZ state
questions.explanation: The three-tangle (or residual tangle) is specifically designed to detect GHZ-type entanglement. It is positive for GHZ-class states and exactly zero for W-class states, making it a key tool in SLOCC classification.

questions.question: In measurement-based quantum computing (MBQC), computation is performed by:
options: Applying a sequence of unitary gates to a product state
Measuring qubits of a cluster state and using feed-forward
Distilling entanglement from noisy states
Teleporting gates between distant qubits
questions.explanation: In MBQC, a highly entangled cluster state is prepared first. Then computation proceeds by adaptive single-qubit measurements. The measurement outcomes, combined with classical feed-forward corrections, implement the desired quantum computation.

questions.question: What does the stabilizer formalism provide for graph states?
options: A way to measure the entanglement entropy
An efficient classical description using Pauli operator generators
A method to convert graph states to GHZ states
A way to make graph states robust to noise
questions.explanation: The stabilizer formalism describes graph states (and other stabilizer states) using a set of n commuting Pauli operators that stabilize the state. This provides an exponentially more efficient classical description and enables efficient simulation of certain quantum circuits.
